user_goals_summary: Writes landing page copy that earns trust through specificity and directness rather than hype. Values a smart-friend voice that treats skeptical, research-savvy readers as adults. Obsesses over CTA architecture — every element should build toward one clear action that feels like the reader's own conclusion.

rubric.name: Conversational authority
rubric.description: The copy sounds like a smart, direct friend — confident without being salesy, occasionally deadpan, never performatively casual. It earns credibility through specificity and tone, not through shouting or wellness-brand enthusiasm.

dimensions.label: Makes claims with quiet confidence rather than hype or exclamation-driven energy
Includes at least one moment of dry, understated delivery that lets the reader draw their own conclusion
Avoids buzzwords, superlatives, and generic wellness-brand language ('revolutionary,' 'game-changing,' 'unlock')
Voice stays at the same tonal register throughout — no sudden shifts between casual and corporate
Key moments land with short, punchy sentences rather than elaborate conclusions

rubric.name: Single-action CTA architecture
rubric.description: The page drives toward exactly one action. Trust is earned before the ask, not after it. The CTA feels like the reader's own conclusion — inevitable, not imposed — with a bridge sentence that makes the button feel like the natural last beat of the copy.

dimensions.label: Only one call-to-action button per section — no competing actions that split attention
All trust-building copy (transparency, credentials, proof) lands before the CTA, not beneath or after it
A bridging sentence immediately before the CTA connects the copy's argument to the action, making the button feel like a conclusion
The CTA reads as the obvious next move rather than a leap of faith or a pitch

rubric.name: Experience-first benefit copy
rubric.description: Benefits are described as felt experiences first — what the reader's body or mind actually notices — with technical specs, doses, and data appearing as supporting proof. The reader walks away with a sensation, not a number.

dimensions.label: Each benefit section opens with what the reader would experience or feel, not with technical specs
Technical details (doses, mechanisms, data) appear after the experiential description, serving as evidence for the felt claim
Uses concrete, body-level language that names specific feelings rather than abstract outcomes ('muscles that won't let go' vs. 'better relaxation')
Avoids listing technical features without connecting each to a felt outcome

rubric.name: Integrated social proof
rubric.description: Customer voices appear woven into the narrative at the exact moment the reader is having the same thought — not in a separate testimonials block. The quote does specific work within its paragraph, confirming a problem or feeling the copy just named.

dimensions.label: Customer quotes or voices appear inside a relevant section, not in a standalone testimonials block
Each customer voice confirms a specific problem, feeling, or experience that the surrounding copy is addressing
The social proof feels like a natural beat in the section's rhythm, not stapled on afterward
Customer quotes name a specific sensation or experience rather than offering generic praise

rubric.name: Skeptic-aware transparency
rubric.description: The copy treats the reader as someone who has been burned by similar products before, reads labels, and is done being marketed to. Transparency is stated as fact within the copy, not positioned as a separate selling point or hidden behind a link.

dimensions.label: Explains mechanisms and trade-offs honestly rather than oversimplifying or hiding complexity
Transparency claims (full label, no proprietary blends) are integrated into the main copy rather than separated into their own destination or footnote
Differentiates from competitors through specific, factual contrasts rather than vague superiority claims or trash-talking
Directly addresses the reader's past bad experiences or reasons for distrust

rubric.name: Full-page coherence
rubric.description: The page reads as one continuous, building argument from top to bottom — not as isolated modules. Each section picks up where the last one left off, voice stays consistent, and no element feels like it was designed separately and assembled later.

dimensions.label: Each section builds on or follows from the previous one rather than starting a new thought from scratch
Tone and register remain consistent across hero, body, and closing sections
Every element (quotes, callouts, subheads) feels integrated into the page's flow rather than floating independently
A top-to-bottom read-through feels like one coherent piece of writing, not assembled modules

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: single CTA per section — competing actions are treated as structural failures, not preference issues. This user diagnoses problems precisely but sometimes needs a few rounds to articulate what's off; presenting options (3-4 variants with brief rationale) accelerates their decision-making. They strongly prefer seeing every revision in context of the full page — isolated section edits create anxiety about coherence. When this user says something 'doesn't quite land,' there's always a specific structural or sequencing issue underneath — ask about order and placement, not just word choice.

rubric.name: Tension-driven openings
rubric.description: Headlines and section openers create a crack in the reader's certainty — a little itch, an unsettled feeling, a challenged assumption. The body copy then resolves that tension. Headlines never state conclusions before the copy has earned them.

dimensions.label: The headline or opener targets something the reader currently believes and puts it in question
The reader feels compelled to read the next line to resolve the tension the headline created
The headline does not state the conclusion or answer — it opens a gap that the body copy closes
The body copy directly resolves or builds on the specific tension the headline introduced

rubric.name: Integrated social proof
rubric.description: Customer voices appear woven into the narrative at the exact moment the reader is having the same thought — not in a separate testimonials block. The quote does specific work within its section, confirming a problem or feeling the copy just named. It should feel earned, not tacked on.

dimensions.label: Customer quotes or voices appear inside a relevant section, not in a standalone testimonials block
Each customer voice confirms a specific problem, feeling, or experience that the surrounding copy is addressing
The social proof feels like a natural beat in the section's rhythm, not stapled on afterward
Customer quotes name a specific sensation or experience rather than offering generic praise

coaching_notes: This user diagnoses structural and tonal problems with exceptional precision but sometimes needs multiple rounds to articulate exactly what's wrong — give them options with clear tradeoff analysis so they can triangulate toward their preference. Non-negotiables: single CTA per section, voice consistency, no marketing-speak. They will always choose specificity over safety and trust the reader's intelligence over explaining too much. When they say something 'feels off,' it almost always is — keep iterating until they stop finding the nagging thing.

writing_type: DTC product and marketing landing page copy
user_goals_summary: Writes conversion-oriented copy for skeptical, analytically-minded audiences. Values a voice that sounds like a smart, direct friend — confident without hype, specific without being clinical. Every element on the page must do identifiable work: openings create tension, benefits lead with felt experience, social proof is woven into narrative, and CTAs feel like the reader's own conclusion.

rubric.name: Tension-driven openings
rubric.description: Headlines and section openers create a crack in the reader's certainty — a little itch, an unsettled feeling, a challenged assumption. The body copy then resolves that tension. Headlines never state conclusions before the copy has earned them.

dimensions.label: The headline or opener targets something the reader currently believes and puts it in question
The reader feels compelled to read the next line to resolve the tension the headline created
The headline does not state the conclusion or answer — it opens a gap that the body copy closes
The body copy directly resolves or builds on the specific tension the headline introduced

rubric.name: Felt experience over abstraction
rubric.description: Any copy beat that aims to create resonance leads with what the reader would actually experience — specific feelings, recognizable moments, concrete behaviors — with technical details or abstract claims appearing only as supporting proof. The reader walks away with something they recognize from their own life, not a vague concept.

dimensions.label: Key copy beats open with what the reader would experience or feel, not with abstract descriptions or technical specs
Technical details, data, or mechanisms appear after the experiential description, serving as evidence for the felt claim
Uses concrete, recognizable details — specific feelings, behaviors, or moments — rather than abstract descriptions ('the second-guessing, the maybe later' vs. 'not being sure if it's worth it')
Every abstract phrase or vague claim is grounded in at least one specific, nameable experience the reader can identify with

rubric.name: Conversational authority
rubric.description: The copy sounds like a smart, direct friend — confident without being salesy, occasionally deadpan, never performatively casual. It earns credibility through specificity and tone, not through hype or wellness-brand enthusiasm. Treats the reader like an adult.

dimensions.label: Makes claims with quiet confidence rather than hype or exclamation-driven energy
Includes at least one moment of dry, understated delivery that lets the reader draw their own conclusion
Avoids buzzwords, superlatives, and generic wellness-brand language ('revolutionary,' 'game-changing,' 'unlock')
Voice stays at the same tonal register throughout — no sudden shifts between casual and corporate
References to competitors or alternatives are specific and factual, never combative or trash-talking

rubric.name: Integrated social proof
rubric.description: Customer voices appear woven into the narrative at the exact moment the reader is having the same thought — not in a separate testimonials block. The quote is the punctuation on a specific feeling, not a floating endorsement. It should feel earned, not tacked on.

dimensions.label: Customer quotes or voices appear inside a relevant section, not in a standalone testimonials block
Each customer voice confirms a specific problem, feeling, or experience that the surrounding copy is addressing
The social proof feels like a natural beat in the section's rhythm, not stapled on afterward
Customer quotes name a specific sensation or experience rather than offering generic praise

rubric.name: Single-action CTA architecture
rubric.description: The page drives toward exactly one action. Trust is earned before the ask, not after it. The CTA feels like the reader's own conclusion — inevitable, not imposed — with a bridge sentence that makes the button feel like the natural last beat of the copy.

dimensions.label: Only one call-to-action button per section — no competing actions that split attention
All trust-building copy (transparency, credentials, proof) lands before the CTA, not beneath or after it
A bridging sentence immediately before the CTA connects the copy's argument to the action, making the button feel like a conclusion
The CTA reads as the obvious next move rather than a leap of faith or a pitch

coaching_notes: This user has sharp diagnostic instincts — they often know something is wrong before they can name exactly what it is. Give them options for high-stakes elements (headlines, openers, bridge lines) but deliver body copy as full drafts. They will always push abstract language toward specificity; never leave a vague emotional claim ungrounded. Competitor references must be factual and generous — the moment copy feels smug or combative, they'll flag it.

dimensions.label: Customer quotes or voices appear inside a relevant section, not in a standalone testimonials block
The quote is introduced with narrative context that makes the reader expect a human voice, rather than appearing cold as a standalone pull quote
Each customer voice confirms a specific problem, feeling, or experience that the surrounding copy is already addressing
